docs.title: Algorithm Overview
docs.text: This page explains the scientific foundation of SnowMapPy's gap-filling algorithm.

docs.title: The Cloud Problem
docs.text:
MODIS (Moderate Resolution Imaging Spectroradiometer) provides daily snow cover observations at 500m resolution. However, cloud contamination significantly reduces usable observations:

Challenge

On average, 40-70% of daily MODIS observations are obscured by clouds, depending on the region and season.

This creates gaps in snow cover time series that must be filled for continuous monitoring.

docs.title: Gap-Filling Strategy
docs.text:
SnowMapPy uses a spatio-temporal gap-filling approach that combines:

  1. Sensor Fusion - Combining Terra and Aqua observations
  2. Temporal Interpolation - Using nearby dates to fill gaps
  3. Spatial Correction - DEM-based elevation thresholds

docs.title: The 6-Day Moving Window
docs.text:
The core algorithm uses a 6-day moving window centered on each target date:

Window: [-3, -2, -1, 0, +1, +2]
        ↑               ↑
     3 days before   2 days after

For each pixel on day t:

  1. Collect observations from days t-3 to t+2
  2. Remove cloud-contaminated values
  3. Apply the selected interpolation method
  4. Output the gap-filled value

Why 6 Days?

The window captures typical cloud clearing timescales while preserving snow dynamics. A larger window would smooth rapid melt events; a smaller window would leave more gaps.

docs.title: Sensor Fusion
docs.text:
MODIS operates on two satellites:

Satellite Equatorial Crossing Time Product Terra ~10:30 AM local solar time MOD10A1 Aqua ~1:30 PM local solar time MYD10A1

SnowMapPy combines both for maximum coverage:

# Fusion logic (simplified)
for each pixel:
    if terra_valid:
        use terra_value  # Morning observation preferred
    elif aqua_valid:
        use aqua_value   # Afternoon as backup
    else:
        mark_as_gap      # Fill later

Why Prefer Terra?

Morning observations (Terra) typically have:

  • Less afternoon convective cloud buildup
  • Lower atmospheric water vapor content
  • More consistent solar illumination geometry

docs.title: Gap-Filling Process
docs.text: After sensor fusion, remaining gaps are filled using temporal interpolation:

docs.title: Step 1: Extract Window Values
docs.text:
For target pixel \((x, y)\) on day \(t\):

\[ W_{t} = \{v_{t-3}, v_{t-2}, v_{t-1}, v_{t}, v_{t+1}, v_{t+2}\} \]

where \(v_i\) is the pixel value on day \(i\).

docs.title: Step 2: Identify Valid Values
docs.text:
Remove gaps and cloud flags:

\[ V_{t} = \{v_i \in W_t : v_i \neq \text{NoData}\} \]

docs.title: Step 3: Interpolate
docs.text: Apply the selected method (nearest, linear, or cubic) using the valid values and their temporal positions.

docs.title: Step 4: Apply Spatial Correction
docs.text: If enabled, adjust the interpolated value based on DEM-derived elevation thresholds.

docs.title: Quality Assurance
docs.text:
SnowMapPy applies QA filtering to remove unreliable observations:

NDSI_Snow_Cover Value Interpretation Action 0-100 Valid NDSI Keep 200 Missing data Mark as gap 201 No decision Mark as gap 211 Night Mark as gap 237 Inland water Preserve 239 Ocean Preserve 250 Cloud Mark as gap 254 Detector saturated Mark as gap 255 Fill value Mark as gap

docs.title: Performance Considerations
docs.text:
The algorithm processes time series pixel-by-pixel using Numba JIT compilation:

@njit(parallel=True)
def process_time_series(data, ...):
    for i in prange(n_pixels):  # Parallel loop
        for t in range(n_times):
            # Process each pixel-time combination
            interpolated[i, t] = interpolate(...)

Performance

Numba JIT compilation provides 50-200x speedup over pure Python/NumPy implementations.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text: See the Scientific Foundation section for the related publication and detailed validation results.

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Interpolation Methods - Choose the right interpolation
  • Spatial Correction - DEM-based adjustments

docs.title: Interpolation Methods
docs.text: SnowMapPy offers three temporal interpolation methods for gap-filling. This guide helps you choose the right one.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Method Speed Smoothness Use Case Nearest Fastest Sharp Binary snow/no-snow analysis Linear Fast Moderate General purpose Cubic Slower Smooth Continuous NDSI values

docs.title: Nearest Neighbor ("nearest")
docs.text: The simplest method - uses the closest valid observation in time.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
Day:    -3   -2   -1    0   +1   +2
Value:  45   --   --    ?   --   60
                        ↓
                       45   (closest to -1)

docs.title: Mathematical Definition
docs.text:
For target time \(t\):

\[ \hat{v}_t = v_{t^*} \quad \text{where} \quad t^* = \arg\min_{i} |t - t_i|, \quad v_i \neq \text{NoData} \]

docs.title: Characteristics
docs.text:
Advantages

  • Fastest - Minimal computation
  • Preserves original values - No artificial values created
  • Works with any number of valid points - Needs only 1 observation

Limitations

  • Discontinuous - Can create abrupt changes
  • No trend estimation - Ignores temporal patterns

docs.title: Recommended For
docs.text:
• Binary snow/no-snow classification
  • Categorical snow cover mapping
  • Maximum processing speed
  • When preserving exact observation values matters

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
result, counters = process_modis_ndsi_cloud(
    project_name="your-project",
    shapefile_path="study_area.shp",
    start_date="2020-01-01",
    end_date="2020-12-31",
    output_path="./output",
    interpolation_method="nearest"  # ← Nearest neighbor
)

docs.title: Linear Interpolation ("linear")
docs.text: Draws a straight line between valid observations to estimate gap values.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
Day:    -3   -2   -1    0   +1   +2
Value:  40   --   --    ?   --   60
                        ↓
                       48   (linear between 40 and 60)

docs.title: Mathematical Definition
docs.text:
Given boundary values \(v_a\) at time \(t_a\) and \(v_b\) at time \(t_b\):

\[ \hat{v}_t = v_a + \frac{v_b - v_a}{t_b - t_a} (t - t_a) \]

docs.title: Characteristics
docs.text:
Advantages

  • Continuous - Smooth transitions between observations
  • Fast - Simple computation
  • Balanced - Good trade-off between speed and quality

Limitations

  • Assumes linear change - May not capture nonlinear melt/accumulation
  • Requires 2 valid points - Falls back to nearest if only 1 available

docs.title: Recommended For
docs.text:
• General-purpose gap filling
  • When temporal continuity matters
  • Moderate accuracy requirements
  • Default choice for most applications

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
result, counters = process_modis_ndsi_cloud(
    project_name="your-project",
    shapefile_path="study_area.shp",
    start_date="2020-01-01",
    end_date="2020-12-31",
    output_path="./output",
    interpolation_method="linear"  # ← Linear interpolation
)

docs.title: Cubic Interpolation ("cubic")
docs.text: Fits a smooth cubic polynomial through valid observations.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
Day:    -3   -2   -1    0   +1   +2
Value:  30   45   --    ?   --   60
                        ↓
                       52   (cubic polynomial fit)

docs.title: Mathematical Definition
docs.text:
Cubic spline interpolation using Catmull-Rom splines:

\[ \hat{v}_t = \sum_{i=0}^{3} c_i(t) \cdot v_i \]

where \(c_i(t)\) are the cubic basis functions.

docs.title: Characteristics
docs.text:
Advantages

  • Smoothest - Continuous first and second derivatives
  • Captures curvature - Better for nonlinear snow dynamics
  • Highest quality - Best for continuous NDSI analysis

Limitations

  • Slowest - More computational overhead
  • Requires 4 valid points - Falls back to linear/nearest if fewer
  • Can overshoot - May produce values outside input range

docs.title: Recommended For
docs.text:
• High-quality NDSI time series
  • Hydrological modeling inputs
  • Snow melt rate estimation
  • When smooth transitions are critical

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
result, counters = process_modis_ndsi_cloud(
    project_name="your-project",
    shapefile_path="study_area.shp",
    start_date="2020-01-01",
    end_date="2020-12-31",
    output_path="./output",
    interpolation_method="cubic"  # ← Cubic interpolation
)

docs.title: Performance Benchmarks
docs.text:
Processing 20 years of daily data (7,300 time steps) over a 1000×1000 pixel area:

Method Processing Time Relative Speed Nearest ~45 seconds 1.0x (baseline) Linear ~60 seconds 0.75x Cubic ~90 seconds 0.5x

All Methods Are Fast

Thanks to Numba JIT compilation, even cubic interpolation completes in reasonable time. Choose based on quality needs, not just speed.

docs.title: Fallback Behavior
docs.text:
When insufficient valid observations exist:

  1. Cubic → Falls back to Linear (if 2-3 points)
  2. Linear → Falls back to Nearest (if 1 point)
  3. Nearest → Uses single available value
  4. No valid points → Remains as gap (NoData)

docs.title: Recommendation Summary
docs.text:
graph TD
    A[Choose Interpolation Method] --> B{Need binary snow maps?}
    B -->|Yes| C[nearest]
    B -->|No| D{High quality required?}
    D -->|Yes| E[cubic]
    D -->|No| F[linear]

Default recommendation: Start with "linear" for most applications.

docs.title: Memory Optimization
docs.text: Process large datasets efficiently with SnowMapPy's memory-optimized architecture.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Processing multi-decadal MODIS time series can require significant memory. SnowMapPy implements several strategies to minimize RAM usage:

Key Benefits

  • Process 20+ years of data on machines with 8GB RAM
  • Handle 1000km×1000km study areas efficiently
  • No intermediate files or temporary storage needed

docs.title: 1. Server-Side Reprojection
docs.text:
Data reprojection happens on Google Earth Engine servers, not locally:

# This happens on GEE servers
image = image.reproject(crs='EPSG:4326', scale=500)
# Only the final, reprojected data is transferred

Impact

  • 50-70% reduction in data transfer
  • No local memory spike during reprojection
  • Faster overall processing

docs.title: 2. Dask Lazy Loading
docs.text:
Data is loaded lazily using Dask, meaning computations are deferred until needed:

import dask.array as da

# Data is not loaded into memory here
data = da.from_zarr('output.zarr')

# Still lazy - no memory used
yearly_mean = data.mean(dim='time')

# Only now is data actually loaded
result = yearly_mean.compute()

Impact

  • Work with datasets larger than available RAM
  • Only load data chunks as needed
  • Automatic chunk optimization

docs.title: 3. Float16 Output
docs.text:
Results are stored in float16 precision by default:

Data Type Bytes per Value NDSI Range Precision float64 8 0-100 Excessive float32 4 0-100 Good float16 2 0-100 Sufficient

Impact

  • 50% reduction in output file size
  • 50% reduction in memory during processing
  • No loss of meaningful precision for NDSI (0-100 range)

docs.title: 4. ZSTD Compression
docs.text:
Zarr output uses ZSTD compression:

# Typical compression ratios
Uncompressed: 1.0x
ZSTD level 3: ~0.4x (60% smaller)

Impact

  • 60% reduction in disk space
  • Fast decompression for analysis
  • Chunked access remains efficient

docs.title: Per Study Area Size
docs.text:
For 1 year of daily data (365 days):

Area Approximate Pixels Peak RAM Usage 100km × 100km 40,000 ~1 GB 500km × 500km 1,000,000 ~4 GB 1000km × 1000km 4,000,000 ~8 GB

docs.title: Per Time Range
docs.text:
For a 500km × 500km area:

Time Range Days Peak RAM Usage 1 month 31 ~0.5 GB 1 year 365 ~4 GB 5 years 1,825 ~8 GB 20 years 7,300 ~12 GB

These Are Estimates

Actual memory usage depends on cloud cover (more gaps = more interpolation work), DEM complexity, and interpolation method.

docs.title: 1. Process in Chunks
docs.text:
For very large datasets, process yearly or seasonally:

import pandas as pd

# Define years to process
years = range(2000, 2023)

for year in years:
    result, counters = process_modis_ndsi_cloud(
        project_name="your-project",
        shapefile_path="study_area.shp",
        start_date=f"{year}-10-01",  # Water year start
        end_date=f"{year+1}-09-30",  # Water year end
        output_path="./output",
        output_name=f"snow_cover_{year}_{year+1}"
    )
    print(f"Completed {year}-{year+1}")

docs.title: 2. Monitor Memory
docs.text:
During processing, monitor your system's memory:

WindowsLinuxPython 
# Task Manager or Resource Monitor
taskmgr

# Real-time monitoring
htop
# Or snapshot
free -h

import psutil

def print_memory():
    mem = psutil.virtual_memory()
    print(f"Used: {mem.used / 1e9:.1f} GB / {mem.total / 1e9:.1f} GB")

docs.title: 3. Close Other Applications
docs.text:
Before processing large datasets:

  • Close memory-intensive applications (browsers, IDEs)
  • Ensure adequate swap/pagefile space
  • Consider running overnight for very large jobs

docs.title: Output Data Type
docs.text:
Control precision vs storage trade-off:

result, counters = process_modis_ndsi_cloud(
    ...,
    output_dtype="float16"    # Default: smallest, fastest
    # output_dtype="float32"  # More precision
    # output_dtype="float64"  # Maximum precision
)

docs.title: Compression
docs.text:
Control compression level:

result, counters = process_modis_ndsi_cloud(
    ...,
    compression="zstd"   # Default: good balance
    # compression="none" # Faster I/O, larger files
)

docs.title: Out of Memory Errors
docs.text:
If you encounter MemoryError:

  1. Reduce study area size - Split into tiles
  2. Reduce time range - Process in yearly chunks
  3. Increase available RAM:
  4. Close other applications
  5. Add more physical RAM
  6. Increase swap/pagefile

docs.title: Slow Processing
docs.text:
If processing is slower than expected:

  1. Check network connection - GEE data transfer depends on bandwidth
  2. Reduce interpolation complexity - Use "nearest" instead of "cubic"
  3. Disable spatial correction - Set spatial_correction_method="none"

docs.text: Understanding and working with SnowMapPy output data.

docs.title: Zarr Output Format
docs.text: SnowMapPy outputs data in Zarr format, a modern cloud-optimized format for N-dimensional arrays.

docs.title: Why Zarr?
docs.text: Feature Benefit Chunked storage Random access without reading entire file Compression 60%+ smaller than uncompressed Cloud-native Direct access from S3, GCS, Azure Lazy loading Works with data larger than RAM Parallel I/O Multiple threads can read simultaneously

├── .zattrs              # Dataset metadata

├── NDSI/
│   ├── .zarray          # Array metadata (shape, chunks, dtype)
│   ├── .zattrs          # Variable attributes
│   ├── 0.0.0            # Chunk files
│   ├── 0.0.1
│   └── ...
├── time/
│   ├── .zarray
│   └── 0
├── x/
│   ├── .zarray
│   └── 0

    ├── .zarray
    └── 0

docs.title: GeoTIFF (Single Time Step)
docs.text:
import xarray as xr
import rasterio
from rasterio.transform import from_bounds

# Load data
ds = xr.open_zarr('output/study_area_NDSI.zarr')

# Select a single day
day_data = ds['NDSI'].isel(time=0).values

# Create transform
transform = from_bounds(
    float(ds.x.min()), float(ds.y.min()),
    float(ds.x.max()), float(ds.y.max()),
    day_data.shape[1], day_data.shape[0]
)

# Write GeoTIFF
with rasterio.open(
    'snow_cover_day1.tif',
    'w',
    driver='GTiff',
    height=day_data.shape[0],
    width=day_data.shape[1],
    count=1,
    dtype='float32',
    crs='EPSG:4326',
    transform=transform,
    compress='lzw'
) as dst:
    dst.write(day_data.astype('float32'), 1)

docs.title: GeoTIFF Stack (Multi-Band)
docs.text:
import xarray as xr
import rasterio
from rasterio.transform import from_bounds
import numpy as np

ds = xr.open_zarr('output/study_area_NDSI.zarr')
data = ds['NDSI'].values  # (time, y, x)

transform = from_bounds(
    float(ds.x.min()), float(ds.y.min()),
    float(ds.x.max()), float(ds.y.max()),
    data.shape[2], data.shape[1]
)

with rasterio.open(
    'snow_cover_stack.tif',
    'w',
    driver='GTiff',
    height=data.shape[1],
    width=data.shape[2],
    count=data.shape[0],  # One band per time step
    dtype='float32',
    crs='EPSG:4326',
    transform=transform,
    compress='lzw'
) as dst:
    for i in range(data.shape[0]):
        dst.write(data[i].astype('float32'), i + 1)

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
Working with Large Data

docs.title: Spatial Correction
docs.text: DEM-based elevation correction improves snow detection accuracy in mountainous regions.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
In mountainous areas, snow distribution is strongly controlled by elevation. Spatial correction uses Digital Elevation Model (DEM) data to apply physically-based constraints to the gap-filling process.

When to Use

Spatial correction is most valuable for:

  • High-relief regions (mountains, plateaus)
  • Areas with strong elevation gradients
  • Studies requiring elevation-specific snow analysis

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
The spatial correction algorithm:

  1. Loads SRTM DEM at 30m resolution from Google Earth Engine
  2. Calculates elevation statistics for the study area
  3. Applies elevation-based thresholds during interpolation

docs.title: Elevation Mean Method
docs.text:
The elevation_mean method adjusts gap-filled values based on the relationship between pixel elevation and mean regional elevation:

# Simplified logic
for each pixel:
    if elevation > regional_mean + threshold:
        # Higher elevation - favor snow presence
        adjust_toward_snow()
    elif elevation < regional_mean - threshold:
        # Lower elevation - favor snow absence
        adjust_toward_no_snow()
    else:
        # Mid-elevation - no adjustment
        keep_interpolated_value()

docs.title: "none" - No Spatial Correction
docs.text:
Uses only temporal interpolation without elevation constraints.

result, counters = process_modis_ndsi_cloud(
    project_name="your-project",
    shapefile_path="study_area.shp",
    start_date="2020-01-01",
    end_date="2020-12-31",
    output_path="./output",
    spatial_correction_method="none"  # ← Disabled
)

When to Use

  • Flat or low-relief study areas
  • Areas without significant elevation gradients
  • Maximum processing speed required

docs.title: "elevation_mean" - Elevation Mean Correction
docs.text:
Applies DEM-based elevation thresholds to constrain gap-filled values.

result, counters = process_modis_ndsi_cloud(
    project_name="your-project",
    shapefile_path="study_area.shp",
    start_date="2020-01-01",
    end_date="2020-12-31",
    output_path="./output",
    spatial_correction_method="elevation_mean"  # ← Enabled
)

When to Use

  • Mountainous regions
  • Studies focused on snow line dynamics
  • When physical constraints improve accuracy

docs.title: DEM Data Source
docs.text:
SnowMapPy uses the SRTM Digital Elevation Model from Google Earth Engine:

Property Value Dataset USGS/SRTMGL1_003 Resolution 30 meters Coverage Global (60°S to 60°N) Vertical accuracy ~16m (absolute)

Server-Side Processing

DEM resampling and reprojection happen on GEE servers, minimizing data transfer and local memory usage.

docs.title: Algorithm Steps
docs.text:
1. DEM Loading 

    # Pseudocode
dem = ee.Image("USGS/SRTMGL1_003")
dem_clipped = dem.clip(study_area)

  2. Statistics Calculation 

    stats = dem_clipped.reduceRegion(
    reducer=ee.Reducer.mean().combine(
        ee.Reducer.stdDev(), sharedInputs=True
    )
)
dem_mean = stats['elevation_mean']
dem_std = stats['elevation_stdDev']

  3. Threshold Definition 

    high_threshold = dem_mean + dem_std
low_threshold = dem_mean - dem_std

  4. Correction Application 

    # During gap-filling kernel
if pixel_elevation > high_threshold:
    if surrounding_high_elevation_pixels_have_snow:
        interpolated_value = adjust_toward_snow(interpolated_value)

docs.title: Numba Implementation
docs.text:
The spatial correction is implemented in the JIT-compiled kernel:

@njit

docs.title: Performance Impact
docs.text:
Correction Method Additional Processing Time none Baseline elevation_mean ~10-15% slower

Why the Overhead?

Additional time comes from:

  • DEM data loading from GEE
  • Elevation statistics calculation
  • Spatial neighborhood analysis during gap-filling

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
Spatial correction has been validated to improve accuracy in mountainous regions:

  • High-relief mountain ranges — 5-8% reduction in commission errors
  • Alpine and subalpine zones — Improved snow line detection
  • Transitional elevations — Reduced noise at forest-alpine boundaries

Regional Applicability

While initially validated in the Atlas Mountains of Morocco (Bousbaa et al., 2024), the elevation-based correction approach is based on universal physical principles and is applicable to any mountainous region worldwide.
